nysed-ela-2014-g6-mc-q1.alt: Question 1. Why are the ponies scared in line 3? Choices: A: They are unable to see where they are headed. B: They are unfamiliar with the world above ground. C: They are uncomfortable being around other ponies. D: They are unsure why they are no longer in the mine.

nysed-ela-2014-g6-mc-q2.alt: Question 2. What is the central theme of the poem? Choices: A: the appeal of discovering the unknown B: the thrill of newfound freedom C: the promise of a well-planned future D: the wonder and beauty of nature

nysed-ela-2014-g6-mc-q3.alt: Question 3. In line 7, why don’t the ponies “understand the grass”? Choices: A: They are sick from breathing in too much coal dust. B: They are too old to remember where they are going and why. C: They have injured legs from pulling heavy coal-trucks. D: They have lived most of their lives in a dark and stony space.

nysed-ela-2014-g6-mc-q4.alt: Question 4. In line 1, what does the simile “like the ghosts of horses” suggest? Choices: A: Living in the mine has made the ponies fierce. B: The ponies are sick because of conditions in the mine. C: Working underground has changed the ponies. D: The ponies look dreadful covered with dust from the mine.

nysed-ela-2014-g6-mc-q5.alt: Question 5. What is the importance of lines 19 through 24? Choices: A: They suggest that the ponies prefer playing to working. B: They show the feelings experienced by one unusual pony. C: They reveal what was once natural behavior for the ponies. D: They indicate that one pony is much smarter than the others.

nysed-ela-2014-g6-mc-q6.alt: Question 6. In line 28, what is the “happy thunder”? Choices: A: the sound of a storm approaching B: the sound of the ponies running C: the sound of the trucks in the mine D: the sound of a pony rolling on the grass

nysed-ela-2014-g6-mc-q7.alt: Question 7. Which detail is most helpful for understanding the central idea of the article? Choices: A: Loggerhead turtles hatch in eastern Florida. B: Loggerhead turtles prefer to feed in warm waters. C: Scientists are interested in protecting animal habitats. D: Scientists have studied how different animals navigate.

nysed-ela-2014-g6-mc-q8.alt: Question 8. Why are lines 9 through 13 important to the article? Choices: A: They show how the turtles hatch eggs. B: They show the types of beaches turtles prefer. C: They explain why the turtles travel long distances. D: They explain why the turtle’s sense of direction is so important.

nysed-ela-2014-g6-mc-q9.alt: Question 9. Why do loggerhead hatchlings have to learn differently from the way many other animals learn? Choices: A: They hatch when it is dark outside. B: They do not have adults to teach them. C: They do not live completely on land. D: They depend on their surroundings.

nysed-ela-2014-g6-mc-q10.alt: Question 10. Scientists conducted experiments to track the movements of baby turtles because they Choices: A: wanted to study how quickly turtles learn new behavior B: were hoping to recreate turtle territories in a laboratory C: were hoping to discover where turtles hatch D: wanted to learn how turtles react to light

nysed-ela-2014-g6-mc-q11.alt: Question 11. According to the article, how might humans threaten loggerhead turtles’ survival in the wild? Choices: A: by creating artificial magnetic fields B: by building bright structures near the ocean C: by preventing turtles from returning to their eggs D: by removing baby turtles from their natural habitat

nysed-ela-2014-g6-mc-q12.alt: Question 12. What is the author’s main purpose for including Dr. Lohmann’s work in the article? Choices: A: to explain to readers how turtles behave in captivity B: to show how Dr. Lohmann conducts his experiments C: to highlight the important role of environment on turtles D: to describe the influence Dr. Lohmann has on the scientific community

nysed-ela-2014-g6-mc-q13.alt: Question 13. Which statement from the article best represents a central idea? Choices: A: “It’s born with a magnetic sense that tells it how to find its way from any place on Earth.” (lines 5 and 6) B: “Loggerheads also seem to have a good memory for places, even for places they have seen just once before.” (lines 9 and 10) C: “In the wild, swimming west would take them the wrong way—away from the ocean.” (lines 47 and 48) D: “Lohmann is working to find other factors that are important in helping sea turtles find their way around the world.” (lines 59 and 60)

nysed-ela-2014-g6-mc-q14.alt: Question 14. Which phrase best explains why people first created carousels? Choices: A: to provide people with a thrilling ride B: as training devices for soldiers C: to display carved animals D: as a game for horses

nysed-ela-2014-g6-mc-q15.alt: Question 15. What made more elaborate carousels possible? Choices: A: new kinds of animals B: a new source of power C: the addition of a chariot D: the inclusion of brass rings

nysed-ela-2014-g6-mc-q16.alt: Question 16. Based on the entire article, the word “lavishly” in line 31 shows that the carousels were Choices: A: faster than they had been B: larger than they had been C: fancier than they had been D: stronger than they had been

nysed-ela-2014-g6-mc-q17.alt: Question 17. How do the details about the Great Depression in lines 40 through 43 contribute to the development of the article? Choices: A: by helping readers form a mental image of an abandoned carousel B: by describing for readers the costs involved in running a carousel C: by creating curiosity about where the carousels were stored D: by providing an explanation for the decline of carousels

nysed-ela-2014-g6-mc-q18.alt: Question 18. What does the history of the word “carousel” reveal about the modern-day carousel? Choices: A: Carousels were once used for training; now they have been put into storage. B: Carousels were once celebrated for their beauty; now they have fallen into disrepair. C: Carousels were once used for military practice; now they are used for entertainment. D: Carousels were once appreciated for their decoration; now they are used for amusement.

nysed-ela-2014-g6-mc-q19.alt: Question 19. Which statement best expresses the author’s point of view in the article? Choices: A: The carousel is an exciting and fascinating piece of history. B: The work to restore and preserve carousels has been completed. C: The first carousels in Europe were more beautiful than later ones. D: The National Carousel Association can do more to save the carousel.
